summary: Tom Adams critiques the Obama Administration's decision to cut FHA mortgage insurance premiums by 0.5%, arguing it will do little to address housing affordability. He points out that mortgage rates are already at historic lows and that the real obstacle to homeownership is stagnant wages relative to home prices, not borrowing costs. The policy primarily benefits new purchasers while existing borrowers face refinancing costs that negate savings. Adams concludes this is less about helping homeowners and more about FHA competing for market share with Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac after their recent policy changes.
key_points: The 0.5% FHA premium reduction saves borrowers roughly $75/month on an average home—unlikely to meaningfully expand homeownership
The real barrier to homeownership is that wages haven't kept pace with home prices, not that credit is too expensive
Existing homeowners wanting prices to rise have strong lobbying power; potential buyers wanting affordable homes have none
The policy is primarily a competitive response to Fannie/Freddie's new 97% loan-to-value offerings, not genuine affordability reform
The Administration's solution to consumer problems continues to be encouraging more debt rather than addressing structural issues
money_quotes: Are they going to increase wages? Or reduce the price of homes? No, they're going to attack mortgage rates for Federal Housing Administration (FHA) borrowers.
If it's already really cheap to borrow money, is another 0.5% reduction going to make that big a difference? Probably not.

Would-be homeowners don't have the income to support buying the houses listed for sale in their markets – which is another way of saying that, for average Americans homes are too expensive for them to afford.

Existing homeowners and home sellers want home prices to go up... On the other hand, potential home buyers would rather not have home prices going up – because that makes buying much harder. For whatever reason, this group has about zero lobbying juice.
It is somewhat disheartening that the Administration still seems to believe that the solution to consumer issues is to have the consumers take on more debt.

The various government housing agencies are competing for market share among the same limited universe of qualifying borrowers by trying to get them to take on bigger mortgages than they would qualify for previously.
It's hard to believe that anyone in the Administration believes that this will do much for making homes more affordable for Americans.
key_insight: Housing affordability policies that focus on cheaper credit rather than addressing the wage-to-home-price gap are ineffective theater that primarily serves government agencies competing for market share.
